questions.question: What does the CAP theorem actually force you to choose between?
options: Any two of consistency, availability, partition tolerance
Consistency or availability, specifically during a network partition
Latency or throughput
SQL or NoSQL
questions.explanation: The precise statement: when a network partition occurs, you must choose between consistency and availability. Partition tolerance isn't optional in a distributed system, so the real choice is C vs A during a partition — not a free 'pick two of three'.

questions.question: Why is partition tolerance considered mandatory for a distributed system?
options: It is the cheapest property to implement
Network partitions are a physical reality that happen whether you 'choose' them or not, so the system must cope with them
Because consistency depends on it
It isn't mandatory; you can always avoid partitions
questions.explanation: Cables fail, switches die, packets drop — partitions will happen. A distributed system can't opt out of them; it can only decide how to behave when one occurs. That's why P is a given and the actual decision is between C and A.

questions.question: During a partition, a read arrives on the side that lacks the latest write. What does a CP system do?
options: Returns the stale value it has
Returns an error or blocks rather than serve possibly-stale data, sacrificing availability
Crashes the whole cluster
Silently makes up a value
questions.explanation: A CP system prioritizes consistency: if it can't guarantee the value is current, it refuses (error or block) rather than return stale data. This sacrifices availability on the affected side. An AP system would instead answer with the possibly-stale value to stay available.

questions.question: Which pairing of data to CAP choice is most appropriate?
options: Account balance → AP; likes count → CP
Cluster leader election → CP; social feed → AP
Everything should always be CP
Everything should always be AP
questions.explanation: Match the choice to the cost of staleness vs downtime. Leader election must be CP — a wrong answer (two leaders) is catastrophic. A social feed can be AP — briefly stale is fine, but being down loses engagement. Balances lean CP; likes counts lean AP.

questions.question: What does PACELC add to CAP?
options: It replaces consistency with availability
It notes that even with no partition (Else), you still trade Latency vs Consistency — strong consistency costs coordination latency
It proves you can have all three of CAP
It only applies to single-node databases
questions.explanation: CAP only covers behavior during a partition. PACELC extends it: if Partitioned, choose A or C; Else, choose Latency or Consistency. Even in normal operation, insisting on strong consistency means waiting for nodes to agree, which adds latency.
